schools.summary: Purpose against sin and the Advocate for sinners — 'my little children' (the aged apostle's tender word); 'that ye sin not' (lest forgiveness be taken as licence, his whole aim is the opposite); 'if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father' (parakletos, one called alongside to plead our cause, lest the fallen despair; the same word as the Spirit, John 14:16); 'Jesus Christ the righteous' (fit Advocate because righteous, a perfect righteousness to present, pleading on his finished sacrifice, Calvin); holiness and provision held together.

Christ the propitiation — 'he is the propitiation for our sins' (hilasmos, the atoning sacrifice appeasing God's wrath and covering sin, Christ both priest and victim, Heb 2:17); 'not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world' (reaching beyond the apostles and believing Jews, Gentile as well as Jew); the Advocate pleading a propitiation of boundless sufficiency.

debates.question: How far does the propitiation 'for the whole world' extend?

sides.position: sufficient for all and effectual for all who by faith embrace the gospel, of every nation — not the universal salvation of every individual
as wide as sin itself, made for all men, though effectual only in those who believe

themes: the sins of the whole world

schools.summary: Obedience the test of knowing God — 'hereby we do know that we know him' (a way to be sure our claim to know God is real); 'if we keep his commandments' (present tense, continual obedience the evidence); striking at the false teachers who boasted knowledge while disregarding God's commands (Clarke); the knowledge of God from the gospel not idle but transforming the life (Calvin); not salvation by works but works as the fruit and evidence of true knowledge.

themes: hereby we know that we know him

schools.summary: The disobedient professor unmasked — 'he that saith, I know him' (professing acquaintance with God, perhaps the Gnostics' boast of superior knowledge, a pious platitude, Robertson) 'and keepeth not his commandments' (the profession belied by a disobedient life); the blunt verdict 'is a liar, and the truth is not in him' (unsoftened); a severe blow against pretenders living in sin and against the Antinomian who divorces knowing Christ from obeying him (Clarke); no knowing God that leaves the commandments unkept.

The love of God perfected in the obedient — 'whoso keepeth his word' (over against the disobedient liar; the indefinite 'whoso,' open to all, not a few initiates, Robertson); 'in him verily is the love of God perfected' (God's love attaining its full effect in the obedient life, or, to keep his word is itself the perfecting of our love to God, Calvin — either way obedience and love inseparable); 'hereby know we that we are in him' (obedience the evidence of union; the tree known by its fruits, Wesley).

themes: hereby know we we are in him

schools.summary: The call to walk as Christ walked — 'he that saith he abideth in him' (claiming the intimate, lasting union with Christ); 'ought himself also so to walk, even as he walked' (bound to order his whole life after Christ's pattern; 'walk' a continuous course of life, not occasional acts, Robertson; Christ's own life of love, holiness, obedience the model); Calvin's logic — not mere exhortation but argument from the union: because joined to him, we ought to be like him, likeness of life the proof of the union.

themes: he that saith he abideth in him

schools.summary: The old commandment of love — the affectionate 'brethren/beloved' (the first such address, fitting a charge about love); 'I write no new commandment' (kainen, not novel in kind; the commandment of brotherly love no strange new doctrine) 'but an old commandment which ye had from the beginning' (heard from the first of their instruction, rooted in the ancient law of love); guarding against the suspicion of novelty, recalling them to a duty always known; resolved with v8 by different senses — old in origin, new in Christ's fresh force.

The commandment also new — the paradox resolved by sense: old in teaching, new in practice and in the fresh form Christ gave it (John 13:34); 'true in him and in you' (the love realized supremely in Christ who loved and died, and reproduced in believers); 'because the darkness is past, and the true light now shineth' (with Christ the true Light come, the old darkness passing, the commandment taking fresh power); God renewing it daily that the faithful practise it lifelong (Calvin); love both ancient and ever-new.

themes: true in him and in you

schools.summary: Hatred the proof of darkness — 'he that saith he is in the light' (professing to be enlightened, in fellowship with the God who is light) 'and hateth his brother' (harbouring positive ill-will toward a fellow-believer); 'is in darkness even until now' (remaining in the darkness he professed to leave, his hatred proving he was never in the light); the claim nullified by hating one's brother, the only way to walk in light being fellowship with the God who is light and love (Robertson); parallel to the test of knowing God by obedience.

themes: he that saith he is in the light

schools.summary: Love the mark of abiding in light — 'he that loveth his brother' (the touchstone, genuine love serving the brother); 'abideth in the light' (menei, continues in God's light, his love not interrupting it as hatred does); 'there is none occasion of stumbling in him' (skandalon — either nothing in him to make others fall or nothing to cause his own fall; love removing the stumbling-blocks hatred sets up); love to the brethren the testimony that we love God (Calvin), the proof one has received Christ (Clarke).

The blinding power of hatred — the threefold plight: 'is in darkness' (his state), 'walketh in darkness' (his whole course of life), 'knoweth not whither he goeth' (lost like a man in the dark, ignorant of his destiny, on the road to hell, Wesley); the cause 'darkness hath blinded his eyes' (etyphlosen, the very verb of Satan blinding unbelievers' minds, 2 Cor 4:4; hatred robbing him of spiritual sight); love and hatred revealed as light and darkness, sight and blindness.

The first assurance — 'I write unto you, little children' (debated whether the youngest believers or, Calvin, the whole family of God of every age; here heading the threefold list); 'because your sins are forgiven you for his name's sake' (the most basic gospel assurance, pardon grounded in Christ's merit and atoning work, not their own worth); John writing not to terrify but to assure his readers as a forgiven people, the bedrock of Christian confidence.

themes: I write unto you, little children

schools.summary: Addresses to the stages of growth — 'fathers' (the mature, rich in experience, knowing 'him that is from the beginning,' the eternal Christ); 'young men' (in spiritual vigour, commended for victory, 'ye have overcome the wicked one'); 'little children' (the youngest, knowing the Father, the first filial knowledge); John enumerating the ages to show his teaching suits every stage (Calvin), the assurance fitted to each — maturity's deep knowledge, youth's victory, infancy's knowledge of the Father.

The assurances repeated and amplified — the fathers addressed again with the same ground ('ye have known him from the beginning,' their mature knowledge needing no addition); the young men more fully — 'ye are strong, and the word of God abideth in you, and ye have overcome the wicked one' (their victory explained by its source: spiritual vigour, the truth taking deep permanent root and moulding them, Clarke, and so conquering Satan); the secret of victory the abiding word, strength coming from the truth dwelling within.

themes: I have written, fathers... young men
the word of God abideth in you
ye have overcome the wicked one

schools.summary: The prohibition of worldly love — 'love not the world, neither the things in the world' (kosmos not the physical creation nor people as objects of compassion but the evil world-system opposed to God, with its allurements; the present prohibition bidding them stop or never form the habit); 'if any man love the world, the love of the Father is not in him' (the two loves cannot coexist; supreme affection on the world proving the Father's love absent); not despising God's creatures rightly used but refusing the inordinate love that makes the world one's idol.

themes: nor the things in the world
the love of the Father not in him

schools.summary: The threefold analysis of worldliness — 'all that is in the world' under three heads: 'the lust of the flesh' (craving for sensual gratification, the bodily appetites), 'the lust of the eyes' (desire through sight — covetousness, love of show and riches grasped for their own sake), 'the pride of life' (alazoneia, the vainglory, boastful self-display, ambition and pomp of status); some seeing the pattern of the first temptation and Christ's; the verdict 'not of the Father, but of the world' — none springing from God.

themes: all that is in the world

schools.summary: The passing world vs the abiding doer of God's will — 'the world passeth away, and the lust thereof' (paragetai, present, the whole system already slipping away, fading and perishing); the folly exposed — to love the world is to set the heart on what is vanishing, wretched provision (Calvin); 'he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever' (the one who obeys God rather than the world endures forever, sharing the permanence of the God he serves, abiding in the eternal enjoyment of what he loves, Wesley); the eternal against the perishing.

The last hour and its antichrists — 'it is the last time' (the final period of redemptive history; expositors differing whether the last age of the world or the closing of the Jewish state, but agreeing it marks a critical hour); 'as ye have heard that antichrist shall come' (the climactic enemy of Christ foretold), 'even now are there many antichrists' (false teachers denying Christ already arisen; Wesley taking it broadly for all such enemies); 'whereby we know it is the last time' — their swarming the proof.
Augustine: since it is the last hour, he bids the children make haste to grow.

The apostasy of the false teachers — 'they went out from us' (once members of the Christian community, professed believers, their defection a real grief); 'but they were not of us' (never truly belonging in heart and life, the phrase shifting from membership to genuine union, Robertson); 'if they had been of us, they would have continued with us' (true belonging shown by perseverance, their falling away proving they were never Christ's; their hearts departed before their feet, Wesley); the apostasy unmasking the false, steadying the weak (Calvin) — the church not discredited by those who leave.

The believers' anointing — against the seducers, 'ye have an unction from the Holy One' (chrisma, given by Christ or God; perhaps set against 'antichrist,' for Christians are the true 'anointed ones,' Robertson; the Holy Spirit and his grace, as Christ the Anointed received the Spirit); 'ye know all things' (the inward teaching giving sufficient knowledge of the truth to preserve from seducers and for salvation, not omniscience but the Spirit's discernment, Wesley); their stability in the day of seducers (Henry).

John's respect for their knowledge — 'I have not written because ye know not the truth' (a courteous disclaimer, not wanting his warnings taken as reckoning them ignorant); 'but because ye know it' (writing to confirm and stir up the knowledge they have, to apply against the seducers); 'no lie is of the truth' (truth and falsehood utterly opposed, no lie springing from truth); conceding them a real power of judgment to discern truth from error (Calvin), the practical discernment the anointing gives.

themes: not because ye know not the truth
no lie is of the truth

schools.summary: The doctrine of antichrist — 'who is a liar but he that denieth that Jesus is the Christ?' (the great lie above all others, denying Jesus is the Messiah; the early heretics, Cerinthus and the Gnostics, acknowledging Jesus as man or teacher but denying he was truly the Christ come in flesh); 'he is antichrist, that denieth the Father and the Son' (to deny the Son is to deny the Father, the two inseparable); 'Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God' one undivided truth, denying any part denying the whole (Wesley).
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Jews allow he is Jesus but not the Christ, hence the apostle's word; Christ a High Priest whose priesthood passes not to another.

themes: denieth that Jesus is the Christ
denieth the Father and the Son

schools.summary: The inseparability of Father and Son — 'whosoever denieth the Son, the same hath not the Father' (rejecting Christ loses the Father too, for the Son alone reveals the Father, John 1:18; 14:9, the only access by the Son, John 14:6); 'he that acknowledgeth the Son hath the Father also' (the two had or lost together); a remarkable sentence among the first axioms of religion — the one true God is no other than the God made known in Christ (Calvin); against the antichrists' empty boast of honouring the Father while denying the Son.
Cyril of Jerusalem: he that denieth the Son hath not the Father; no one comes to the Father but through Christ the Door.

The call to abide in the original gospel — the emphatic 'ye,' set against the seducers; 'let that abide in you which ye have heard from the beginning' (the original apostolic message about the Father and Son fixed and rooted, no novelty added, no part lost); the promise 'ye also shall continue in the Son, and in the Father' (perseverance in the doctrine securing perseverance in the fellowship); the reciprocity — the word abiding in them, they abiding in God; the antidote to seduction not new revelations but the old gospel kept.

themes: continue in the Son and the Father

schools.summary: The promise of eternal life — linked to abiding (v24): 'this is the promise that he hath promised us, even eternal life' (the supreme gift, life everlasting in fellowship with God, promised to those who abide); the great encouragement to hold fast — maintaining the truth, they have the sure promise; departing, as the antichrists, it is forfeited; 'the promise' explained, as John explains his terms, as the eternal life that was with the Father and was manifested (1:2); belonging to the persevering (Wesley).

The purpose of the warning — 'these things have I written' (the section on the antichrists, vv18-25) 'concerning them that seduce you' (planonton, conative present, the false teachers actively trying to lead the believers astray, the attempt in progress); John forearming his readers, exposing the denial of the Son so the sheep are not led off; writing not as though his words alone sufficed but to stir them to seek the Spirit's guidance (Calvin); the faithful shepherd's pastoral love.

The abiding anointing as teacher — against the seducers, 'the anointing which ye have received of him abideth in you' (the Spirit, v20, permanent and faithful); 'ye need not that any man teach you' (not dependent on the seducers or those claiming secret knowledge, the Spirit teaching 'of all things' truly); the qualification pressed lest abused — not that all human teaching is needless (John himself teaches as he writes), but the anointed do not need the Gnostic pretenders' false instruction; this does not exclude being taught by those sharing the same anointing (Wesley).

debates.question: Does 'ye need not that any man teach you' abolish human teaching?

sides.position: no — it denies the need of the Gnostic seducers' false teaching; it does not exclude being taught by those who share the same anointing (John himself is teaching them)

themes: ye need not that any man teach you

schools.summary: The call to abide unto his appearing — 'and now, little children' (John speaking again to all his readers); 'abide in him' (continue in living union with Christ, gathering up all the foregoing); the motive from the future 'when he shall appear' (a clear reference to the second coming) 'we may have confidence, and not be ashamed' (the twofold prospect — those who abided meeting him with boldness, those who have not shrinking back in shame); the modest 'we,' John including himself; present abiding made urgent by the certainty of Christ's return.

Righteousness the mark of the new birth — 'if ye know that he is righteous' (taking as certain that God/Christ is righteous, perfectly holy; John speaking of Father and Son so as one that the pronoun could mean either, though 'born of him' points to the Father, Ellicott); 'every one that doeth righteousness is born of him' (whoever practises righteousness must be a child of God, bearing the Father's likeness); the first argument that faith is joined to a holy life — begotten after God's likeness, his righteousness reappearing in his children (Calvin); not perfection but the settled practice of righteousness.

themes: if ye know that he is righteous
